one_line_takeaway: Auto-fetched URL metadata can silently drive an agentic LLM (qwen2.5:7b) to exfiltrate secrets via tool calls in 88% of runs while its visible response stays benign in 95% of those cases; sharding cuts single-request leakage detectability by 73% (Leak@1), and only network/system-layer controls (domain allowlisting, redirect detection), not prompt-layer defenses, drove egress to 0% in the ablation.
key_finding: Baseline aggregate P(egress) = 0.881 (423/480 runs) with 0.0% false positives across 120 benign control runs. Direct single-shot delivery achieved P(egress)=1.000 for Body, Title, and Anchor surfaces and 0.967 for Meta. In 95.0% of successful attacks, the exfiltration occurred purely through network egress while the agent's final textual response remained benign -- meaning output-based safety checks would miss the large majority of successful attacks. Sharded exfiltration (splitting a secret into 4 fragments) cut Leak@1 from 0.967 to 0.263 (a 73% reduction) and Leak@3 from 0.967 to 0.158 (84% reduction), but also lowered raw P(egress) success across surfaces by 17-37% (largest drop on Meta, -37%) due to the added multi-step instruction complexity. In the defense ablation: prompt-layer defenses were weak -- a hardened system prompt reduced P(egress) from 1.000 to 0.667, delimiter tags gave no measurable benefit (1.000, +/-0%), and combining both only reached 0.567. Network-layer controls were fully effective in this setting -- domain allowlisting and redirect-chain/TLD/path detection each drove P(egress) to 0.000 (-100%), and content-inspection/DLP methods (keyword filter, entropy detection) likewise reached 0.000, with all-defenses-combined yielding zero observed exfiltration attempts in the sample.
core_contribution: The paper defines 'implicit prompt injection' as a subclass of indirect prompt injection in which adversarial instructions enter an LLM agent's context not through content the user explicitly retrieves, but through automatic system behaviors such as URL previewing and metadata extraction (titles, meta descriptions, Open Graph/Schema.org tags) that the user never sees. It builds a fully local, reproducible testbed (a Python agent driving a qwen2.5:7b model served via Ollama, an attacker-controlled collection endpoint, and a malicious page server with configurable injection payloads) and runs 480 experiments across 16 configurations (4 injection surfaces x 2 delivery methods x 2 exfiltration modes, 30 trials each) to measure 'silent egress' -- outbound network requests that exfiltrate sensitive runtime context while the agent's final user-facing text response stays benign. It also introduces and evaluates 'sharded exfiltration' (splitting a secret into multiple fragments sent across separate requests to evade per-request inspection), and runs a defense ablation (n=30 per condition, 270 runs) comparing prompt-layer, network-layer, and content-inspection (DLP) defenses.
threat_model: System model: a standard ReAct-style agentic LLM (context composer aggregating high-privilege user/system input and low-privilege external content such as URL previews into one flat context window; a tool interface exposing web_request GET/POST, file_read, search; a network egress channel to arbitrary/partially restricted endpoints). Adversary: external, unprivileged, black-box -- controls public web content (HTML, Open Graph/Schema.org metadata, HTTP headers), may use redirect chains or SEO poisoning, has no knowledge of the system prompt, model weights, or user history, and cannot modify the agent's source code, intercept encrypted traffic, or inject directly into chat history. Security goal: confidentiality of runtime context (user secrets, conversation history, system instructions) against tool-mediated side-channel exfiltration. The authors explicitly frame the attack as a confused-deputy problem (agent holds legitimate network/tool authority but is manipulated by lower-privileged web content) combined with an LLM-mediated SSRF variant (the model's reasoning, not direct parameter control, constructs the malicious request).
limitations: The evaluation is explicitly attacker-favorable to establish a clean baseline: sensitive data is placed directly and visibly in context, the system prompt is minimal, and the tool-calling format is explicit -- all of which raise attack success relative to a hardened real deployment. Experiments use a single open-weight model (qwen2.5:7b) and only HTTP-based egress; the authors did not test proprietary production systems (GPT-4/Claude/Gemini) to avoid ToS/legal risk, and explicitly caution (rather than claim) that stronger instruction-following in larger models could increase compliance with injected instructions, making their numbers a plausible lower bound rather than an upper bound. DNS exfiltration and timing side channels were not evaluated. The ablation study covers only one configuration (body/direct/single) and one clearly-external attacker domain (evil-collector.net); the authors themselves note that domain allowlisting is far harder in practice against compromised legitimate domains, subdomain takeovers, or open redirects, and that adaptive adversaries could evade the all-combined defense stack via obfuscation or alternative channels not tested here.

datasets_used: Fully local synthetic testbed: attacker-controlled collection endpoint + configurable malicious web page server generating adversarial HTML/metadata payloads (no external benchmark dataset used)
quantitative_results: 480 total experimental runs across 16 configurations (4 surfaces x 2 delivery x 2 exfiltration modes, 30 trials each)
Aggregate P(egress) = 0.881 (423/480 runs); baseline P(egress) 0.88 direct delivery vs 0.89 redirect chains
Direct single-shot P(egress) by surface: Body 1.000, Title 1.000, Anchor 1.000, Meta 0.967
95.0% silent attack rate (successful exfiltration with benign final text response)
False positive rate: 0.0% across 120 benign control runs
Sharded exfiltration: Leak@1 drops from 0.967 (single-shot) to 0.263 (sharded), a 73% reduction; Leak@3 drops from 0.967 to 0.158, an 84% reduction
Sharded P(egress) by surface vs single-shot: Body 1.00->0.77 (-23%), Meta 0.97->0.60 (-37%), Title 1.00->0.83 (-17%), Anchor 1.00->0.83 (-17%)

Defense ablation (n=30/condition, 270 runs total): Baseline P(egress)=1.000; System prompt hardening -> 0.667 (-33.3%); Delimiter tags -> 1.000 (+/-0%); Both combined -> 0.567 (-43.3%); Domain allowlist -> 0.000 (-100%, 100% blocked); Redirect detection -> 0.000 (-100%, 100% blocked); Keyword filter -> 0.000 (-100%, but only 23.3% attempt rate detected/triggered); Entropy detection -> 0.000 (-100%, 0% attempt rate); All combined -> 0.000 (no exfiltration attempts observed)
baselines_compared: Benign-page control condition (120 runs, no adversarial content) used as false-positive baseline
Prompt-layer defenses: system-prompt hardening, delimiter/tag wrapping of external content, both combined
Network-layer defenses: domain allowlisting, redirect-chain/suspicious-TLD/path detection
Content-inspection (DLP) defenses: keyword filtering, Shannon-entropy detection on URL parameters
